Andrej Karpathy \u2013 Blog.text:
**New this period:**

- The microgpt project simplifies large language models to their bare essentials and is available on GitHub and as a Google Colab notebook.
- The project uses a dataset of 32,000 names and a simple tokenizer that assigns one integer to each unique character in the dataset.

**Details:**
**[microgpt](http://karpathy.github.io/2026/02/12/microgpt/)**
- The core thesis of this piece is the release of microgpt, a simplified large language model implemented in a single Python file.
- Companies mentioned: GitHub, Google (for Google Colab notebook).
- The specific role of GitHub is to host the microgpt source code, and the specific role of Google is to provide the platform for the Google Colab notebook.
- Quantitative figures: 200 lines of code, 32,000 names in the dataset, 26 possible lowercase characters (a-z) in the tokenizer, and 1 special token (BOS) for a total vocabulary size of 27.
- Supply chain or value chain relationships: the project relies on the GitHub platform for hosting the code and the Google Colab notebook for interactive execution.
- Technology transitions: microgpt simplifies large language models by reducing them to their essential components, making it easier to understand and implement these models.
- The project uses a simple tokenizer and autograd engine, which are key components of large language models, and demonstrates their usage in a simplified setting.
- The autograd engine is implemented using a Value class that wraps scalar numbers and tracks how they were computed, allowing for automatic differentiation and gradient computation.
- No specific capital allocation signals or regulatory signals are mentioned in this piece.

Ethan Mollick \u2013 One Useful Thing.text:
**New this period:**
- AI models are improving at an accelerating rate, with evidence showing capability gains in areas such as software development and complex problem-solving.
- The way people use AI is changing, with a shift from co-intelligence to using long-running, smart, and self-correcting AI systems, or agents.
- Agents are being adopted across various functions, including non-tech roles, and are changing the nature of work, with users increasingly acting as managers of AI systems.

**Details:**
**The twilight of the chatbots (https://www.oneusefulthing.org/p/the-twilight-of-the-chatbots)**
- The article discusses the rapid improvement of AI models, with leading American AI labs such as Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google releasing more powerful models at an accelerating rate.
- The author mentions that government interventions have temporarily stopped access to two of the most powerful models, Claude Fable and GPT-5.6.
- Quantitative figures include:
  - Opus 4.7 was able to build a software package in 14 hours that would take 2-17 weeks of human engineering work, at a cost of $251 in tokens.
  - Fable was able to work autonomously for 9 hours to execute complex software projects that would have taken a team over a week to do.
- Supply chain and value chain relationships include:
  - American companies Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google are leading the development of frontier AI models.
  - Chinese companies are developing near-frontier AI models, which are open weights models that can be used or modified by anyone after release.
- Technology transitions include:
  - A shift from using AI as a co-intelligence to using long-running, smart, and self-correcting AI systems, or agents.
  - The development of harnesses and apps, such as Claude Code or OpenAI's Codex, that give AI access to tools and an environment to act in.
- Competitive positioning:
  - American companies are currently leading the development of frontier AI models, but Chinese companies are catching up with near-frontier models.
  - The use of agents is changing the nature of work, with users increasingly acting as managers of AI systems, and experts in various domains are able to use agents effectively.
- Capital allocation signals:
  - The author mentions a joint study by OpenAI and academic economists, which shows the rapid adoption of agents within OpenAI.
  - A separate study of Claude Code users found that software engineers had a similar success rate to other professions when using Claude Code on coding tasks.
- Regulatory or policy signals:
  - Government interventions have temporarily stopped access to two of the most powerful AI models, Claude Fable and GPT-5.6.
  - The author mentions that AI is not capable of being a real cybersecurity threat until suddenly it is, causing sudden and improvised policy changes at the highest level of government.

Arxiv \u2013 Trading & Market Microstruc.text:
**New this period:**
- Systematic trend following has ceased to deliver reliable returns since approximately 2009, particularly for short-term trends.
- The liquidity-normalised tick size is a key factor in distinguishing between degraded and surviving trends in futures contracts.
- The price-impact coefficient can be estimated directly from daily equity order flow and used to forecast stock returns.
- Institutional liquidity entering prediction markets can potentially tighten spreads, improve price discovery, and benefit traders, but its effects may vary depending on market conditions.

**Details:**
**Is Trend Still Your Friend?: A Microstructural Account of the Demise of Short-Term Trend-Following** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01550)
- The study examines the decline of short-term trend-following strategies in futures contracts since 2009.
- A cross-section of approximately 100 liquid futures contracts spanning 1995-2025 is used, along with an industry-representative CTA (Commodity Trading Advisor) proxy.
- The volatility-normalised tick size is identified as a key factor in distinguishing between degraded and surviving trends, with small-tick contracts experiencing a collapse in trend profitability.
- The study suggests that the post-2008 transition to HFT (High-Frequency Trading)-dominated market making has broken the self-fulfilling feedback loop that sustains trend-following strategies on small-tick contracts.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study implies that CTAs and HFT firms play a role in the trend-following ecosystem.

**Details:**
**Liquidity Premium and Investment Horizons** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.01377)
- The study estimates the price-impact coefficient (λ) directly from daily equity order flow using CRSP data from 2020 to 2025.
- The study finds that signed order flow strongly predicts contemporaneous and one-month-ahead stock returns, while volume volatility predicts lower subsequent returns.
- The study uses two estimators of λ: a within-month price-impact regression and an Amihud-style ratio.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study uses CRSP data, which is a widely used database of stock prices and trading volumes.

**Details:**
**What Happens When Institutional Liquidity Enters Prediction Markets: Identification, Measurement, and a Synthetic Proof of Concept** (https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.10005)
- The study examines the potential effects of institutional liquidity on prediction markets, including the tightening of spreads and improvement of price discovery.
- The study uses a synthetic microstructure laboratory as a proof of concept for measuring the impact of institutional liquidity on market quality.
- The study suggests that market-maker coverage, liquidity incentives, and automation can have varying effects on market quality and trader welfare, depending on market conditions.
- No specific companies are mentioned, but the study implies that institutional liquidity providers and market makers play a role in shaping market outcomes.

Arxiv \u2013 Computational Finance (q-fi.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers propose using Shapley values to explain financial language models, aiming to provide more transparent and domain-knowledge-aligned explanations.
- A new index, the Aggregated Systemic Risk Index (ASRI), is introduced to measure systemic risk in cryptocurrency markets, combining four transmission channels into a single composite.

**Details:**
**Shapley in Context: Explaining Financial Language with Domain Expertise**(https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.00856)
- The study explores the use of Shapley values to explain large language models in financial applications, focusing on aligning explanations with established financial domain knowledge.
- No specific companies are mentioned in the study.
- The research aims to address the need for explainability in finance, where regulatory requirements and high stakes demand transparent models.
- The study employs rigorous theoretical analysis and extensive empirical evaluations to demonstrate the effectiveness of Shapley values in explaining financial textual data.

**ASRI: An Aggregated Systemic Risk Index for Cryptocurrency Markets**(https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.03874)
- The Aggregated Systemic Risk Index (ASRI) is a measure of crypto-native systemic stress, aggregating four transmission channels: stablecoin concentration, DeFi liquidity, cross-market contagion, and regulatory opacity.
- No specific companies are mentioned in the study, but the index is evaluated against four crypto crises: Terra/Luna, Celsius/3AC, FTX, and SVB.
- The study reports the following quantitative figures:
  - AUROC values: 0.866 (ASRI), 0.851 (Contagion channel), 0.858 (first principal component of ASRI's channels), and 0.875 (equity-volatility series, VIX).
  - p-values: approximately 0.04-0.06 for the three large-signal events, with Terra/Luna being non-significant.
- The ASRI composite nests its individual channels, providing interpretive value through channel attribution, lead-time, and regime structure.
- The study uses a Diebold-Yilmaz connectedness series as the network benchmark for the contagion channel.

FinAnEx \u2013 SUFE Finance Research For.text:
**New this period:**
- Researchers discover that "prediction bias" is a unified mechanism explaining individual investor stock trading decisions, including buying, selling, and funding allocation.
- A study of 959 Danish investors finds that those with higher prediction bias tend to buy stocks with higher past returns and sell stocks with lower capital gains.
- The study's findings suggest that prediction bias affects market timing behavior, with investors with higher bias increasing funding allocations after strong market performance.

**Details:**
**Extrapolators And Contrarians: Forecast Bias And Individual Investor Stock Trading** (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfineco.2026.104291)
- The research question is how prediction bias affects individual investor stock trading decisions.
- The method involves a controlled laboratory experiment with 959 Danish investors, where they predict a randomly generated time series and receive rewards based on accuracy.
- The key finding is that prediction bias, measured as a core parameter, significantly influences buying, selling, and funding allocation decisions.
- Named companies and their roles are not mentioned in the study.
- Quantitative figures include:
  - 959: number of Danish investors participating in the experiment
  - 11 years: duration of the administrative trading records analyzed
  - 3.0 percentage points: average increase in excess return for stocks purchased by investors with higher prediction bias
  - 2011-2021: time period of the trading records analyzed
- Supply chain and value chain relationships are not explicitly described in the study.
- Technology transitions are not mentioned in the study.
- The study's findings suggest that investors with higher prediction bias tend to buy stocks with higher past returns and sell stocks with lower capital gains, while those with lower bias tend to do the opposite.
- The study's results have implications for understanding individual investor behavior and market timing.

The Batch \u2013 Andrew Ng (DeepLearning.text:
**New this period:**
- OpenAI launched a preview of its GPT-5.6 family, including a top-tier model comparable to Anthropic's Claude 5 Mythos, but with limited access to U.S. government-approved organizations.
- OpenAI's GPT-5.6 models achieved strong performance in coding, cybersecurity, and biology, with GPT-5.6 Sol setting the state of the art on Terminal-Bench 2.1.
- The U.S. government has begun to control the launch of top-performing AI models, with OpenAI limiting the launch of GPT-5.6 to around 20 government-approved organizations.

**Details:**
**The Batch – Andrew Ng (DeepLearning.AI)**(https://www.deeplearning.ai/the-batch/issue-360)
- OpenAI announced a preview of its GPT-5.6 family, including GPT-5.6 Sol, GPT-5.6 Terra, and GPT-5.6 Luna, with limited access to U.S. government-approved organizations.
- Companies mentioned: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, Cerebras, DeepLearning.AI.
- Roles: OpenAI (developer of GPT-5.6 models), Anthropic (developer of Claude Mythos 5 model), Cerebras (will offer access to GPT-5.6 Sol at speeds up to 750 tokens per second).
- Quantitative figures: 
  - GPT-5.6 Sol price: $5/$0.50/$30 per 1 million input/cached/output tokens.
  - GPT-5.6 Terra price: $2.50/$0.25/$15 per 1 million input/cached/output tokens.
  - GPT-5.6 Luna price: $1/$0.10/$6 per 1 million input/cached/output tokens.
  - GPT-5.6 Sol performance: 91.9% on Terminal-Bench 2.1, 88.8% on Terminal-Bench 2.1 (unspecified reasoning level), 73.5% on ExploitBench, 96.7% on OpenAI's internal capture-the-flag security challenges.
  - SecureBio test results: GPT-5.6 Sol scored 68.3% on World-Class Bio, a nearly 10-point improvement over GPT-5.5 (59.7%).
- Supply chain and value chain relationships: OpenAI partnered with the U.S. government to limit the launch of GPT-5.6 to approved organizations.
- Technology transitions: The GPT-5.6 family brings concerns about AI's security implications to the lower-priced tier, with cheaper and faster models drawing more scrutiny.
- Competitive positioning: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol outperformed Anthropic's Claude Mythos 5 on Terminal-Bench 2.1, but was narrowly outperformed by Claude Mythos Preview on ExploitBench.
